declarations.doc: Freshman's dream for the linear factor: in characteristic `p`, `(X - C a)^(p^f) = X^(p^f) - C (a^(p^f))`. This is the polynomial form of the identity that turns the BCIKS factor `(Y - P)^(p^f)` into a `p^f`-expanded shape.
declarations.name: ArkLib.sub_C_pow_expChar_pow

declarations.doc: `expand` of a linear factor: `expand K (p^f) (X - C a) = X^(p^f) - C a`. Note this holds over any commutative semiring (no characteristic hypothesis needed); it is the "untwisted" companion of `sub_C_pow_expChar_pow`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.expand_X_sub_C

declarations.doc: The Frobenius/`expand` bridge specialised to the linear factor: applying the iterated Frobenius coefficient-wise to the `p^f`-expansion of `X - C a` recovers the `p^f`-power `(X - C a)^(p^f)`. This is `Polynomial.map_iterateFrobenius_expand` phrased for the honest factor and is the precise statement that "the `p^f`-power factor is the Frobenius image of an expanded factor".
declarations.name: ArkLib.X_sub_C_pow_expChar_pow_eq_map_expand

declarations.doc: The roots (with multiplicity) of `(X - C a)^n` form the multiset `n • {a}`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.roots_X_sub_C_pow

declarations.doc: A `p^f`-power factor `(X - C a)^(p^f)` has its unique root at `a`, with multiplicity exactly `p^f`. Over a domain this is the precise sense in which the honest value `a` (`= P` in the BCIKS factor `(Y - P)^(p^f)`) is read off from the factor.
declarations.name: ArkLib.X_sub_C_pow_factor_roots
declarations.signature: theorem X_sub_C_pow_factor_roots {p f : ℕ} [ExpChar K p] (a : K) :

declarations.doc: The root multiplicity of `a` in the `p^f`-power factor is exactly `p^f`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.rootMultiplicity_X_sub_C_pow

declarations.doc: `(X - C a)^n` is separable iff `n = 1`. (`n = 0` gives `1`, which *is* separable, but the `↔` is for `n` ranging over the "factor" exponents that occur; we phrase the clean directional facts separately below.)
declarations.name: ArkLib.separable_X_sub_C_pow_iff
declarations.signature: theorem separable_X_sub_C_pow_iff [Nontrivial K] {a : K} {n : ℕ} (hn : n ≠ 0) :

declarations.doc: For `n ≥ 2` (the genuinely-repeated case, e.g. `n = p^f` with `f > 0`), `(X - C a)^n` is *not* separable. This is the obstruction that distinguishes a true `p^f`-power factor from the honest linear factor.
declarations.name: ArkLib.not_separable_X_sub_C_pow
declarations.signature: theorem not_separable_X_sub_C_pow [Nontrivial K] {a : K} {n : ℕ} (hn : 2 ≤ n) :

declarations.doc: The `p^f`-expanded power `(X - C a)^(p^f)` is separable iff its exponent is `1`. This is the characteristic-free, always-true core.
declarations.name: ArkLib.separable_X_sub_C_expChar_pow_iff
declarations.signature: theorem separable_X_sub_C_expChar_pow_iff (p f : ℕ) [ExpChar K p] (a : K) :

declarations.doc: In genuine prime (exponential) characteristic `p`, the `p^f`-power factor `(X - C a)^(p^f)` is separable iff `f = 0`. Equivalently (via `sub_C_pow_expChar_pow`) `X^(p^f) - C (a^(p^f))` is separable iff `f = 0`: an *inseparable* factor signals a non-trivial Frobenius twist `f > 0`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.separable_X_sub_C_prime_pow_iff
declarations.signature: theorem separable_X_sub_C_prime_pow_iff (p f : ℕ) [ExpChar K p] (hp : p.Prime) (a : K) :

declarations.doc: For a genuine Frobenius twist `f > 0` in prime characteristic, the `p^f`-power factor is *not* separable: this is the obstruction distinguishing a true `p^f`-power factor from the honest linear factor `X - C a`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.not_separable_X_sub_C_prime_pow
declarations.signature: theorem not_separable_X_sub_C_prime_pow (p f : ℕ) [ExpChar K p] (hp : p.Prime)

declarations.doc: If two `p^f`-power factors agree, `(X - C a)^(p^f) = (X - C b)^(p^f)` with the exponent `p^f` nonzero, then their honest values agree: `a = b`. This says the map `a ↦ (X - C a)^(p^f)` is injective, so the value `P` in the BCIKS factor `(Y - P)^(p^f)` is uniquely determined by the factor.
declarations.name: ArkLib.eq_of_X_sub_C_pow_eq
declarations.signature: theorem eq_of_X_sub_C_pow_eq {p f : ℕ} [ExpChar K p]

This is exactly the shape L7's recursion consumes, established for the genuine in-tree objects with the `W`-divisibility coming from `Hypotheses` (no sorry/axiom).  Requires `σ + 1 ≤ R.natDegree` (so that the cleared exponent `R.natDegree − σ − 1` and the matching `(R.natDegree − σ − 1) + 1` index are the
declarations.name: ArkLib.lineHasseCoeff_hasWPowerNumerator

The natDegree bound (input (a) of `genHasseCoeff_hasWPowerNumerator_of_clearing`) is proven outright via `genHasseDerivNumerPoly_natDegree_le
declarations.name: ArkLib.genHasseCoeff_hasWPowerNumerator_of_dvd_top

declarations.doc: The key denominator-clearing divisibility: if the `F[X]`-coefficient `W^j` divides `a`, then `embedding(C a)/W^j` lies in the integral part of `𝕃`.  This is the `B/W^j ∈ 𝒪` fact (in the form where the numerator is a bivariate `C`-coefficient), specialised to the `W = leadingCoeff` denominator the A.4 coefficients use.
declarations.name: ArkLib.regularElms_set_C_div_W_pow_of_dvd

declarations.doc: `A : 𝕃 H` has a `W`-power numerator at exponent `j`, i.e. `A = B / W^j` with `B ∈ 𝒪`, expressed in the denominator-cleared form `A · W^j = embedding B`.  (When `W` is invertible in `𝕃` — which it is, `W ≠ 0` — this is equivalent to `A = embedding B / W^j`.)
declarations.name: ArkLib.HasWPowerNumerator
declarations.signature: def HasWPowerNumerator {H : F[X][Y]} (A : 𝕃 H) (j : ℕ) : Prop :=

declarations.doc: The integral elements are exactly the numerator forms at exponent `0`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.hasWPowerNumerator_zero_iff

declarations.doc: Any integral element has a numerator form at every exponent (multiply through by `W^j`).
declarations.name: ArkLib.hasWPowerNumerator_of_regular

declarations.doc: The numerator at exponent `0` of an integral element is itself.
declarations.name: ArkLib.hasWPowerNumerator_embedding

declarations.doc: A `W`-power itself has a numerator form: `W^a = (W^{a+j}) / W^j`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.hasWPowerNumerator_W_𝕃_pow

declarations.doc: **Product bookkeeping.**  If `A₁ = B₁/W^{j₁}` and `A₂ = B₂/W^{j₂}` then `A₁·A₂ = (B₁·B₂)/W^{j₁+j₂}`: the numerators multiply and the `W`-exponents add.  This is the L7 step that multiplies recursion factors.
declarations.name: ArkLib.hasWPowerNumerator_mul
declarations.signature: lemma hasWPowerNumerator_mul {H : F[X][Y]} {A₁ A₂ : 𝕃 H} {j₁ j₂ : ℕ}

declarations.doc: **`W`-multiplication bookkeeping.**  Multiplying a numerator form by `W^a` *lowers* the effective denominator exponent by `a` (when `a ≤ j`): `W^a · (B/W^j) = B/W^{j−a}`.  This realises the `W^{i₁+δ}` and `ξ^{2i₁+Σλ−2}` prefactor absorption in recursion (A.1).
declarations.name: ArkLib.hasWPowerNumerator_W_𝕃_pow_mul
declarations.signature: lemma hasWPowerNumerator_W_𝕃_pow_mul {H : F[X][Y]} {A : 𝕃 H} {j a : ℕ}

declarations.doc: **Numerator forms are integral, given the `𝒪`-side divisibility.**  In general `A · W^j = embedding B` only *determines* `A = embedding B / W^j`; this quotient is integral precisely when `W^j ∣ B` *inside `𝒪`* (equivalently, the genuine A.4 numerator carries the divisibility witness `B_{i₁,λ} = W^{…}·(integral)` — App.-A line 2931, the `W ∣ leadingCoeff Rx0` save).  Under that witness the term lands back in `𝒪`.  This is the honest, kernel-clean closure fact L7 threads: each recursion term, being a numerator form whose numerator the recursion supplies already divisible by the relevant `W`-powe
declarations.name: ArkLib.hasWPowerNumerator.mem_regularElms_set_of_dvd

declarations.doc: The same closure, phrased on the packaged `HasWPowerNumerator` shape together with the `𝒪`-side divisibility witness produced by the recursion.  This is the lemma L7 calls per recursion term.
declarations.name: ArkLib.hasWPowerNumerator.mem_regularElms_set

This is the genuine plumbing through which the App-A `W`-prefactor divisibility propagates.

declarations.doc: **Main discharge.**  From the single genuine structural hypothesis `hdvd_C : (C W) ∣ R.coeff R.natDegree` (the App-A vanishing/multiplicity structure of the GS factor `R` at `x₀`, with `W = H.leadingCoeff`), the residual `hdvd_top` holds for **every** inner Hasse order `i₁`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.hdvd_top_of_dvd_C

declarations.doc: The structural hypothesis `hdvd_C` implies the `i₁ = 0` line fact `W ∣ (R.coeff R.natDegree).eval (C x₀)` — so it is a genuine *strengthening* of (consistent with) the `Hypotheses`-supplied line divisibility, not an orthogonal assumption.
declarations.name: ArkLib.hdvd_C_implies_zero_case

declarations.doc: For `i₁ = 0` the residual `hdvd_top` is discharged outright from `Hypotheses` (no residual).

declarations.doc: **General `W`-power-numerator theorem with the residual discharged.**  For *every* inner Hasse order `i₁`, given the single structural hypothesis `hdvd_C : (C W) ∣ R.coeff R.natDegree`, the general mixed Hasse-derivative coefficient `A_{i₁,σ}` has the App-A `W`-power-numerator form at exponent `R.natDegree − σ − 1`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.genHasseCoeff_hasWPowerNumerator_of_dvd_C

declarations.doc: **L7-facing entry point with the residual discharged.**  The general coefficient lands in `𝒪` (`∈ regularElms_set H`) given the structural hypothesis `hdvd_C` and the recursion's `𝒪`-side divisibility witness on its numerator — matching L7's `hA`/`Bcoeff` interface, with no `hdvd_top` residual.
declarations.name: ArkLib.genHasseCoeff_mem_regularElms_set_of_dvd_C

declarations.doc: If `a` and `b` are both congruent to `a₀` modulo `X` (membership in `span {X}`), then so are they to each other: `X ∣ a - b`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.X_dvd_sub_of_sub_mem_span

declarations.doc: **Hensel / Newton root uniqueness over `k⟦X⟧`.** If `f : (k⟦X⟧)[Y]` has two roots `a`, `b`, both congruent modulo `X` to a common approximation `a₀` at which the derivative `f'(a₀)` is a unit (the root is *simple* at `a₀`), then `a = b`. This is the uniqueness companion to `powerSeries_hensel_lift` / `powerSeries_newton_root` (existence).
declarations.name: ArkLib.hensel_root_unique

declarations.doc: **Existence-and-uniqueness over `k⟦X⟧`** (`HenselianLocalRing` route): a monic `f` with an approximate simple root `a₀` has a *unique* exact root congruent to `a₀` modulo `X`.  Combines `powerSeries_hensel_lift` (existence) with `hensel_root_unique` (uniqueness).
declarations.name: ArkLib.powerSeries_hensel_lift_unique

declarations.doc: **The lifted root equals the constructive Newton root.**  The exact root produced by the constructive Newton sequence (`powerSeries_newton_root`) coincides with *any* root congruent to the approximation `a₀` modulo `X` — uniqueness ties the abstract existence to the explicit sequence. Concretely: if `a` is the constructive Newton root (`witness` of `powerSeries_newton_root`) and `b` is any root congruent to `a₀` mod `X`, then `a = b`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.newton_root_eq_of_isRoot
declarations.signature: theorem newton_root_eq_of_isRoot (f : Polynomial (PowerSeries k)) (a₀ : PowerSeries k)

declarations.doc: **Constructive existence-and-uniqueness over `k⟦X⟧`** (Newton route): the explicit Newton-limit root is the *unique* root congruent to `a₀` modulo `X`.  No monicity required — the unit derivative powers both existence and uniqueness.
declarations.name: ArkLib.powerSeries_newton_root_unique
declarations.signature: theorem powerSeries_newton_root_unique (f : Polynomial (PowerSeries k)) (a₀ : PowerSeries k)

declarations.doc: The coefficient ring hom `evalRingHom (C x₀) ∘ C : F[X] →+* F[X]` is the identity: `C a` is the constant in `(F[X])[X]`, and evaluating the outer variable at `C x₀` returns `a`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.HliftDvd.evalRingHom_comp_C

declarations.doc: **The specialization bridge.**  Specializing the lift `Hlift H = H.map C` at the middle variable `X = x₀` recovers `H` exactly: `evalX (C x₀) (Hlift H) = H`.  This is what makes `Hlift H ∣ R` an *un-specialization* of the in-tree specialized datum `H ∣ evalX (C x₀) R`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.HliftDvd.evalX_Hlift

declarations.doc: **Main discharge — factor-membership form.**  If the lift `Hlift H` is a normalized factor of the GS interpolant `R` (the un-specialized App-A factor relation, in the *same* `normalizedFactors` shape as the in-tree `pg_Rset`), then `Hlift H ∣ R`.  Real `UniqueFactorizationMonoid.dvd_of_mem_normalizedFactors`; this is exactly the residual brick `MultiplicityDatum.hdvd_C_of_Hlift_dvd` consumes.
declarations.name: ArkLib.HliftDvd.Hlift_dvd_of_mem_normalizedFactors

declarations.doc: **Main discharge — associated-to-a-factor form.**  The `pg_Rset` factors are *normalized* representatives, so the App-A factor of `R` is in general only *associated* to `Hlift H`.  From `r ∈ normalizedFactors R` with `Associated (Hlift H) r`, the divisibility follows.
declarations.name: ArkLib.HliftDvd.Hlift_dvd_of_associated_mem_normalizedFactors

declarations.doc: **Main discharge — cofactor form.**  The directly-structural reading: the App-A factorization `R = Hlift H * G` of the interpolant with cofactor `G` gives `Hlift H ∣ R`.  (`Dvd.intro`.)
declarations.name: ArkLib.HliftDvd.Hlift_dvd_of_cofactor

declarations.doc: **Consistency.**  From the un-specialized `Hlift H ∣ R`, specialization at `X = x₀` recovers the proven in-tree datum `H ∣ evalX (C x₀) R` (`BCIKS20AppendixA.ClaimA2.Hypotheses.dvd_evalX`).  So the factor-membership hypothesis of the discharges above implies the proven specialized fact — it is the App-A lift, not the goal in disguise.
declarations.name: ArkLib.HliftDvd.dvd_evalX_of_Hlift_dvd

declarations.doc: Packaged consistency: the factor-membership hypothesis (`Hlift H ∈ normalizedFactors R`) discharges the proven in-tree specialized divisibility `H ∣ evalX (C x₀) R`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.HliftDvd.dvd_evalX_of_mem_normalizedFactors

declarations.doc: **Obligation-1 end-to-end discharge.**  From the App-A factor membership `Hlift H ∈ normalizedFactors R`, the structural divisibility `(C H.leadingCoeff) ∣ R.coeff R.natDegree` in `(F[X])[X]` (obligation 1) holds — combining this file's `Hlift_dvd_of_mem_normalizedFactors` with `MultiplicityDatum.hdvd_C_of_Hlift_dvd`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.HliftDvd.hdvd_C_of_factorMembership

declarations.doc: **Atomic converse step.**  If the specialization `π_z z root` of `β` at a matching point `z` (with rational-root data `root`) vanishes, then `z` lies in the matching-point set `S_β β`. This is just unfolding the definition `S_β β = {z | ∃ root, π_z z root β = 0}`, packaging the *concrete* witnessing section `root` into the existential.
declarations.name: ArkLib.IngredientC.mem_S_β_of_pi_z_eq_zero

declarations.doc: **The isolated property `P(β)`.**  For a matching set `matchingSet : Finset F` with a rational-root section `root`, `MatchingVanishes` says the specialization of `β` vanishes at every matching point.  This is the *exact* requirement of ingredient C, stated as a clean hypothesis (no `sorry`): it is the strongest possible "`matchingSet ⊆ S_β β`" — a concrete witnessing section for each point's `S_β`-membership existential. `L13`'s real β-construction will discharge `MatchingVanishes` via Hensel uniqueness (`pi_z_eq_zero_of_specialization_eq` below).
declarations.name: ArkLib.IngredientC.MatchingVanishes

declarations.doc: **The converse bridge (L14 packaging).**  The isolated property `P β` (`MatchingVanishes`) implies that the whole matching set sits inside `S_β β`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.IngredientC.matchingSet_subset_S_β_of_P

declarations.doc: **Ingredient C, conditional formalization (the deliverable).** Given: * `hP : MatchingVanishes matchingSet root β` — the isolated property `P β`, i.e. the specialization `π_z z (root z) β` vanishes at every geometric matching point `z` (this is what L13's real `β` will provide via Hensel uniqueness); * `hcard : (#matchingSet : WithBot ℕ) > weight_Λ_over_𝒪 hH β D * H.natDegree` — the matching set is larger than the `Λ·d` weight bound (this is what L9/L10/L13's weight bound + §5 largeness give); we conclude `embeddingOf𝒪Into𝕃 _ β = 0`. This reduces **all** of ingredient C to producing a `β` sati
declarations.name: ArkLib.IngredientC.embedding_eq_zero_of_matchingSet_large

declarations.doc: **Per-point step, equality form.**  If the specialization of `β` at the matching point equals a quantity `e` that is already known to vanish, then `π_z z root β = 0`.  Trivial, but it is the exact landing pad: L14 produces `e = 0` (the proximate-root polynomial `P_z` minus its truncation) from Hensel uniqueness, and this records that `π_z z root β` inherits the vanishing.
declarations.name: ArkLib.IngredientC.pi_z_eq_zero_of_eq_zero
declarations.signature: lemma pi_z_eq_zero_of_eq_zero {H : F[X][Y]} (β : 𝒪 H) {z : F}

declarations.doc: **Per-point step via Hensel uniqueness.** This is the concrete way L14 discharges a single matching point.  Suppose: * `f : (k⟦X⟧)[Y]` is the matching polynomial at `z` (the §5 specialization `R(X, ·, Z)` recentered at the matching coordinate, with `k = F`); * `aβ` is the power-series specialization of `β` at `z` and `aP` is the proximate-root power series `P_z` (both are *roots* of `f`); * both are congruent mod `X` to the common §5 approximation `a₀` (the degree-0 simple root `α₀`), at which `f'(a₀)` is a unit (separability of `R`). Then Hensel uniqueness (`hensel_root_unique`) forces `aβ =
declarations.name: ArkLib.IngredientC.specialization_eq_proximate_root_of_hensel

declarations.doc: **Vanishing on the close-codeword graph (the GS multiplicity ⟹ root step).** If the bivariate interpolant `Qz : F[X][Y]` vanishes to Guruswami–Sudan order `m` at every graph point `(ωᵢ, Pz(ωᵢ))` of the close codeword `Pz` over an agreement set `A`, and the univariate specialisation `Qz.eval Pz` has degree strictly below the Johnson threshold `m · #A`, then `Qz.eval Pz = 0`: the interpolant *vanishes on the graph of the close codeword*. This is the trivariate `Q_vanishes_on_close_codeword_graph` keystone re-derived standalone from GS primitives: `orderAt_eval_ge` turns each order-`m` graph poin
declarations.name: ArkLib.MatchingExtractor.eval_eq_zero_of_orderM_and_count

declarations.doc: **The matching-polynomial extraction (the GS root ⟹ divisibility step).** From the same Guruswami–Sudan order-`m` vanishing data and Johnson count, the interpolant `Qz` is divisible by the **matching factor** `Y − Pz`: `(Polynomial.X - Polynomial.C Pz) ∣ Qz`. `Polynomial.X` here is the outer variable `Y` of `F[X][Y]` and `Polynomial.C Pz` is the close codeword polynomial embedded as a constant in `Y`; `Y − C Pz` is the graph of `Pz`.  This is the factor of [BCIKS20] §5 bound to the point `z`: the per-point *matching polynomial*.
declarations.name: ArkLib.MatchingExtractor.matchingFactor_dvd_of_orderM_and_count

declarations.doc: The matching-correctness predicate for a single close codeword: the polynomial `Pz` is the matching polynomial bound to the interpolant `Qz` by the graph-vanishing `Qz.eval Pz = 0`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.MatchingExtractor.MatchesGraph
declarations.signature: def MatchesGraph (Qz : F[X][Y]) (Pz : F[X]) : Prop := Qz.eval Pz = 0

declarations.doc: `MatchesGraph` is equivalent to divisibility by the matching factor `Y − Pz`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.MatchingExtractor.matchesGraph_iff_dvd

declarations.doc: **Matching-polynomial extraction, packaged.**  From the GS order-`m` vanishing of `Qz` at the close-codeword graph over an agreement set `A`, under the Johnson count, there *exists* a matching polynomial — namely `Pz` itself — satisfying `MatchesGraph Qz Pz`.  This is the existential datum a `MatchingExtractor` consumes, here delivered constructively from the multiplicity hypothesis.
declarations.name: ArkLib.MatchingExtractor.matchingPolynomial_extracts

declarations.doc: The Johnson count from the GS weighted-degree budget: if `deg Pz ≤ k` and the GS `(1, k)`-weighted degree of the interpolant `Qz` is strictly below `m · #A`, then the univariate specialisation `Qz.eval Pz` is below the Johnson threshold, so the matching factor `Y − Pz` divides `Qz`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.MatchingExtractor.matchingFactor_dvd_of_weightedDegree

declarations.doc: **Finite-family GS list-size bound from order-`m` graph vanishing.** For every candidate message `P ∈ Ps`, assume `Qz` has GS order-`m` vanishing on the graph of `P` over the agreement set `A P`, and that the specialized degree is below the corresponding root-count budget.  Then all those candidates contribute distinct linear factors of `Qz`, so their number is at most the `Y`-degree of `Qz`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.MatchingExtractor.list_size_le_of_orderM_and_count

declarations.doc: **Finite-family GS list-size bound from the weighted-degree Johnson budget.** This is the common caller-facing form: the degree of every candidate is at most `k`, and the Johnson count is discharged from the `(1,k)` weighted-degree bound on `Qz`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.MatchingExtractor.list_size_le_of_orderM_and_weightedDegree

declarations.doc: **The multiplicity ⟹ `HasOrderAt` conversion (single point).**  From the *proven* root multiplicity lower bound `m ≤ rootMultiplicity Q x y`, the GS order-`m` vanishing predicate `GuruswamiSudan.HasOrderAt Q x y m` holds.  This is the easy direction of the multiplicity criterion, derived from the public `GuruswamiSudan.rootMultiplicity_le_of_coeff_ne_zero`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.MultiplicityDatum.hasOrderAt_of_rootMultiplicity_ge
declarations.signature: theorem hasOrderAt_of_rootMultiplicity_ge [DecidableEq F] {Q : F[X][Y]} {x y : F} {m : ℕ}

declarations.doc: **Obligation-4 datum, packaged over an agreement set.**  From the *proven* per-index root multiplicity `m ≤ rootMultiplicity Qz (ωs i) (Pz.eval (ωs i))` (the field-side output of `gsQ_multiplicity` / the `gapB_transport_mult` transport behind `Q_vanishes_on_close_codeword_graph`) on an agreement set `A`, the exact `hord` hypothesis consumed by `MatchingExtractor.matchingFactor_dvd_of_orderM_and_count` is produced: `∀ i ∈ A, GuruswamiSudan.HasOrderAt Qz (ωs i) (Pz.eval (ωs i)) m`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.MultiplicityDatum.hord_of_rootMultiplicity_ge

declarations.doc: **GS matching-factor extraction from bivariate root-multiplicity data.** If the interpolant has root multiplicity at least `m` on the agreement graph points of `Pz`, and the specialized degree is below the corresponding root-count budget, then `Y - Pz(X)` divides the interpolant.
declarations.name: ArkLib.MultiplicityDatum.matchingFactor_dvd_of_rootMultiplicity_and_count

declarations.doc: Weighted-degree form of `matchingFactor_dvd_of_rootMultiplicity_and_count`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.MultiplicityDatum.matchingFactor_dvd_of_rootMultiplicity_and_weightedDegree

declarations.doc: **Finite-family GS list-size bound from bivariate root-multiplicity data.** For every candidate `P ∈ Ps`, assume the interpolant has root multiplicity at least `m` on the agreement graph points indexed by `A P`, and that the specialized degree is below `m * #(A P)`. Then the candidate family has size at most the `Y`-degree of the nonzero interpolant.
declarations.name: ArkLib.MultiplicityDatum.list_size_le_of_rootMultiplicity_and_count

declarations.doc: Weighted-degree form of `list_size_le_of_rootMultiplicity_and_count`.  The per-candidate specialized degree budget is discharged from `P.natDegree ≤ k` and the common `(1,k)` weighted degree bound on `Qz`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.MultiplicityDatum.list_size_le_of_rootMultiplicity_and_weightedDegree

declarations.doc: Embed `H : F[X][Y]` into `F[X][X][Y]` by lifting each (inner-`X`) coefficient with `C`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.MultiplicityDatum.Hlift

declarations.doc: The lift `Hlift H` has the same `Y`-natDegree as `H` (`C` is injective, so no leading term is killed).
declarations.name: ArkLib.MultiplicityDatum.natDegree_Hlift
declarations.signature: lemma natDegree_Hlift (H : F[X][Y]) : (Hlift H).natDegree = H.natDegree := by

declarations.doc: The leading `Y`-coefficient of `Hlift H` is `C (H.leadingCoeff)`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.MultiplicityDatum.leadingCoeff_Hlift

declarations.doc: Over the domain `(F[X])[X]`, the leading `Y`-coefficient of a divisor divides that of the dividend (`leadingCoeff` is multiplicative over a domain).
declarations.name: ArkLib.MultiplicityDatum.leadingCoeff_dvd_of_dvd
declarations.signature: lemma leadingCoeff_dvd_of_dvd {a b : F[X][X][Y]} (hdvd : a ∣ b) :

declarations.doc: **Main obligation-1 discharge.**  From the un-specialized GS-factor divisibility `Hlift H ∣ R` in `F[X][X][Y]` (the genuine §5/App-A factorization structure, whose leading `Y`-coefficient is `C H.leadingCoeff`), the residual structural hypothesis `hdvd_C : (C H.leadingCoeff) ∣ R.coeff R.natDegree` consumed by `ArkLib.hdvd_top_of_dvd_C` holds — **as a theorem**, not a hypothesis.
declarations.name: ArkLib.MultiplicityDatum.hdvd_C_of_Hlift_dvd

declarations.doc: **Value-projection datum, no residual.**  From the proven `Hypotheses x₀ R H` structure (`H ∣ evalX (C x₀) R` and `evalX (C x₀) R` separable), the value at `C x₀` of `hdvd_C` holds: `W ∣ (R.coeff R.natDegree).eval (C x₀)`.  This is the `i₁ = 0` line input of `hdvd_top_zero`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.MultiplicityDatum.hdvd_C_value_of_hypotheses

declarations.doc: Consistency: the un-specialized `hdvd_C` (from `Hlift H ∣ R`) implies its value projection, matching `ArkLib.hdvd_C_implies_zero_case`.  So the obligation-1 theorem is a genuine strengthening of the `Hypotheses`-backed `i₁ = 0` line fact, not an orthogonal assumption.
declarations.name: ArkLib.MultiplicityDatum.hdvd_C_of_Hlift_dvd_implies_value

declarations.doc: The trivial partition `λ^(t)` (`indiscrete t`) has exactly one part. (`Σλ = 1`.)
declarations.name: ArkLib.Nat.Partition.card_parts_indiscrete

declarations.doc: The single part of `λ^(t)` is `t` itself.
declarations.name: ArkLib.Nat.Partition.mem_parts_indiscrete

declarations.doc: A partition of `t` whose multiset of parts contains `t` is the trivial partition `λ^(t)`. This is the structural fact behind the decreasing measure: only `λ^(t)` uses the part `t`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.Nat.Partition.eq_indiscrete_of_mem_self
declarations.signature: theorem eq_indiscrete_of_mem_self (ht : 0 < t) {p : Nat.Partition t} (hm : t ∈ p.parts) :

declarations.doc: **The key export for L7's well-founded recursion (`i₁ = 0` case).** For a partition `p` of `t` that is *not* the trivial partition `λ^(t)` (`indiscrete t`), every part `m ∈ p.parts` satisfies `m < t` strictly. This is precisely the BCIKS20 App-A.4 fact that the recursion `β_t = Σ_{λ ≠ λ^(t)} … ∏_l β_l^{λ_l}` only refers to `β_l` with `l < t`, so the recursion on (A.1) is well-founded. (DAG §6: "in any `λ ≠ λ^(t)` every `l` with `λ_l>0` has `l < t`".)
declarations.name: ArkLib.Nat.Partition.parts_lt_of_ne_indiscrete

declarations.doc: The `i₁ > 0` branch of the recursion strict-decrease: every part of a partition of `t - i₁` (with `0 < i₁ ≤ t`) is `< t`, automatically — no exclusion of `λ^(t)` is needed because the parts already sum to `t - i₁ < t`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.Nat.Partition.parts_lt_of_pos_sub
declarations.signature: theorem parts_lt_of_pos_sub {i₁ : ℕ} (hi₁ : 0 < i₁) (hit : i₁ ≤ t)

declarations.doc: **The `Σλ ≥ 2` export (non-trivial case).** A partition of `t > 0` that is not `λ^(t)` has at least two parts (`Σλ = Multiset.card p.parts ≥ 2`). Used by L8 to show `2i₁ + Σλ − 2 ≥ 0` (so the `ξ`-exponent of every term of (A.1) is non-negative; the only delicate case is `i₁ = 0`).
declarations.name: ArkLib.Nat.Partition.two_le_card_parts_of_ne_indiscrete

declarations.doc: **The combined strict-decrease lemma for the `(A.1)` recursion step.** In the `β_{t+1}` step the outer sum ranges over `(i₁, λ)` with `i₁ ≤ t+1`, `λ : Partition (t+1−i₁)`, excluding only the trivial pair `(0, λ^(t+1))`; the recursive calls are `β_l` for `l ∈ λ.parts`. This lemma certifies every such `l` satisfies `l < t + 1`, which is exactly the obligation discharged by `termination_by β_rec t => t` / `decreasing_by`. The hypothesis `hexcl` excludes precisely the single forbidden term `(i₁ = 0, λ = λ^(t+1))`, expressed type-safely via the parts multiset (`λ^(t+1)` is the unique partition with
declarations.name: ArkLib.Nat.Partition.recursionStep_parts_lt

declarations.doc: The well-founded relation underlying the `β`-recursion: ordinary `<` on `ℕ`, applied to the recursion index `t`. Provided so callers can write a `WellFoundedRelation`-based definition; the strict decrease of each recursive call is `recursionStep_rel`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.Nat.Partition.recursionWF

declarations.doc: Restatement of the combined strict decrease in terms of `recursionWF`: every recursive index `l` of the `β_{t+1}` step is related to `t + 1` by `recursionWF.rel` (i.e. `l < t + 1`).
declarations.name: ArkLib.Nat.Partition.recursionStep_rel

declarations.doc: `Polynomial.degreeLT R 0 = ⊥`: the only polynomial with degree strictly less than `0` (in `WithBot ℕ`) is the zero polynomial. Not `@[simp]` to avoid disrupting existing simp-based proofs that unfold `degreeLT` directly.
declarations.name: Polynomial.degreeLT_zero

declarations.doc: Helper: membership in `(Ideal.span {X})^n • (⊤ : Submodule (k⟦X⟧) (k⟦X⟧))` is the same as `X^n ∣ ·`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.mem_span_X_pow_smul_top

declarations.doc: `k⟦X⟧` is `X`-adically precomplete: every coherent sequence has a limit.
declarations.name: ArkLib.powerSeries_isPrecomplete

declarations.doc: `k⟦X⟧` is `X`-adically complete.
declarations.name: ArkLib.powerSeries_isAdicComplete_span_X

declarations.doc: `k⟦X⟧` is `X`-adically complete with respect to its maximal ideal.
declarations.name: ArkLib.powerSeries_isAdicComplete_maximalIdeal

declarations.doc: `k⟦X⟧` is a Henselian local ring.
declarations.name: ArkLib.powerSeries_henselianLocalRing

declarations.doc: **Simple-root Hensel lift in `k⟦X⟧`** (the concrete `(X)`-adic form of `is_henselian`). A monic `f` over `k⟦X⟧` with an approximate root `a₀` (`f.eval a₀ ≡ 0 mod X`) whose derivative is a unit at `a₀` has an exact root `a` congruent to `a₀ mod X`.  This is the Newton/Hensel step underlying [BCIKS20] Appendix A.4: a power-series lift in the variable `X` over the coefficient field.
declarations.name: ArkLib.powerSeries_hensel_lift

declarations.doc: The power-series ring over the rational function field `F(Z) = RatFunc F` is Henselian. This is the **exact base ring of the [BCIKS20] Appendix-A.4 Hensel lift** used in the §5 list-decoding argument to extract the curve coefficient polynomials — i.e. the foundation of "ingredient D" of the proximity-gap keystone: power series in the lift variable `X` over the coefficient field `K = F(Z)`.  It is a free instance of `powerSeries_henselianLocalRing`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.ratFunc_powerSeries_henselianLocalRing

declarations.doc: One Newton step: `step f a = a - f(a) · (f'(a))⁻¹`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.newtonStep
declarations.signature: def newtonStep (f : Polynomial (PowerSeries k)) (a : PowerSeries k) :

declarations.doc: The Newton sequence with seed `a₀`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.newtonSeq
declarations.signature: def newtonSeq (f : Polynomial (PowerSeries k)) (a₀ : PowerSeries k) :

declarations.name: ArkLib.newtonSeq_zero
declarations.signature: theorem newtonSeq_zero (f : Polynomial (PowerSeries k)) (a₀ : PowerSeries k) :

declarations.name: ArkLib.newtonSeq_succ
declarations.signature: theorem newtonSeq_succ (f : Polynomial (PowerSeries k)) (a₀ : PowerSeries k) (n : ℕ) :

declarations.doc: If `X ∣ a - b` then `X` divides the difference of evaluations.
declarations.name: ArkLib.X_dvd_eval_sub
declarations.signature: theorem X_dvd_eval_sub (p : Polynomial (PowerSeries k)) {a b : PowerSeries k}

declarations.doc: The constant coefficient of `p.eval` is invariant under congruence mod `X`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.constantCoeff_eval_eq
declarations.signature: theorem constantCoeff_eval_eq (p : Polynomial (PowerSeries k)) {a b : PowerSeries k}

declarations.doc: Being a unit at `a` is preserved by congruence mod `X`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.isUnit_eval_of_X_dvd
declarations.signature: theorem isUnit_eval_of_X_dvd (p : Polynomial (PowerSeries k)) {a b : PowerSeries k}

declarations.doc: `φ ∈ (span {X})^n • ⊤ ↔ X^n ∣ φ` (the membership consumed by `IsPrecomplete`).
declarations.name: ArkLib.mem_span_X_pow_smul_top'

declarations.doc: **Core Newton estimate.** If `f'(a)` is a unit then `f(a)² ∣ f(step a)`. This is the algebraic heart of quadratic convergence: by Taylor expansion `f(a + h) = f(a) + f'(a)·h + c·h²` with `h = -f(a)·(f'(a))⁻¹`, the first-order term cancels `f(a)` exactly, leaving a multiple of `f(a)²`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.newtonStep_sq_dvd
declarations.signature: theorem newtonStep_sq_dvd (f : Polynomial (PowerSeries k)) (a : PowerSeries k)

declarations.doc: The seed satisfies `X ∣ f(a₀)`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.newtonSeq_base_dvd
declarations.signature: theorem newtonSeq_base_dvd (f : Polynomial (PowerSeries k)) (a₀ : PowerSeries k)

declarations.doc: The combined invariant maintained along the Newton sequence: the derivative stays a unit **and** `X ^ (2 ^ n) ∣ f(aₙ)`. These two facts are mutually dependent (the divisibility uses the unit to take a Newton step; the unit is preserved because the step moves `aₙ` by a multiple of `X`, which needs `X ∣ f(aₙ)`), so we prove them together by a single induction.
declarations.name: ArkLib.newtonSeq_invariant
declarations.signature: theorem newtonSeq_invariant (f : Polynomial (PowerSeries k)) (a₀ : PowerSeries k)

declarations.doc: The derivative stays a unit along the whole Newton sequence.
declarations.name: ArkLib.newtonSeq_deriv_isUnit
declarations.signature: theorem newtonSeq_deriv_isUnit (f : Polynomial (PowerSeries k)) (a₀ : PowerSeries k)

declarations.doc: **Quadratic convergence**: `X ^ (2 ^ n) ∣ f(aₙ)`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.newtonSeq_quadratic
declarations.signature: theorem newtonSeq_quadratic (f : Polynomial (PowerSeries k)) (a₀ : PowerSeries k)

declarations.doc: Consecutive iterates agree modulo an increasing power of `X`: `X ^ (2 ^ n) ∣ a (n+1) - a n`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.newtonSeq_step_dvd
declarations.signature: theorem newtonSeq_step_dvd (f : Polynomial (PowerSeries k)) (a₀ : PowerSeries k)

declarations.doc: Any iterate is congruent to `a₀` modulo `X`: `X ∣ a n - a₀`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.newtonSeq_sub_base_dvd
declarations.signature: theorem newtonSeq_sub_base_dvd (f : Polynomial (PowerSeries k)) (a₀ : PowerSeries k)

declarations.doc: For `p ≤ q`, `X ^ (2 ^ p) ∣ a q - a p`: the tail of the sequence is captured to high `X`-adic precision.
declarations.name: ArkLib.newtonSeq_dvd_of_le
declarations.signature: theorem newtonSeq_dvd_of_le (f : Polynomial (PowerSeries k)) (a₀ : PowerSeries k)

declarations.doc: The Newton sequence is `(X)`-adically coherent (a Cauchy / `SModEq` chain). We use the coarse modulus `X ^ p ∣ a q - a p`, which is implied by the sharp quadratic estimate and is exactly what `IsPrecomplete` consumes.
declarations.name: ArkLib.newtonSeq_isCoherent
declarations.signature: theorem newtonSeq_isCoherent (f : Polynomial (PowerSeries k)) (a₀ : PowerSeries k)

declarations.doc: **The constructive limit of the Newton sequence.** There is an explicit power series `a∞` (the `X`-adic limit of `newtonSeq f a₀`) which is an exact root of `f` and congruent to `a₀` modulo `X`.  The limit is produced by the precompleteness instance `ArkLib.powerSeries_isPrecomplete`, applied to the coherent Newton chain.
declarations.name: ArkLib.newtonSeq_limit
declarations.signature: theorem newtonSeq_limit (f : Polynomial (PowerSeries k)) (a₀ : PowerSeries k)

declarations.doc: **Constructive Newton/Hensel lift in `k⟦X⟧`.**  This matches `ArkLib.powerSeries_hensel_lift`, but the root is the *explicit* `X`-adic limit of the recursive Newton sequence `newtonSeq f a₀`, with the quadratic-convergence estimate `X ^ (2 ^ n) ∣ f(aₙ)` available (`newtonSeq_quadratic`) for degree tracking — exactly what the BCIKS20 Appendix-A.4 lift needs. Hypotheses: `f.eval a₀ ≡ 0 mod X` and `f'(a₀)` a unit.  Monicity is **not** required (unlike the `HenselianLocalRing` route): the unit derivative is what powers the construction.
declarations.name: ArkLib.powerSeries_newton_root
declarations.signature: theorem powerSeries_newton_root (f : Polynomial (PowerSeries k)) (a₀ : PowerSeries k)

declarations.doc: A power series with zero constant coefficient has `n`-th coefficient of its `d`-th power equal to zero whenever `n < d`: `g^d` has order `≥ d`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.coeff_pow_eq_zero_of_lt_of_constantCoeff_zero

declarations.doc: The `n`-th coefficient of `(X * h) ^ d` for `n < d` vanishes; the `g = X * h` packaging of the positive-order hypothesis.
declarations.name: ArkLib.coeff_X_mul_pow_eq_zero_of_lt

declarations.doc: The coefficient-of-substitution finsum (`PowerSeries.coeff_subst'`), re-exposed in the `ArkLib` namespace for the γ-bookkeeping.
declarations.name: ArkLib.coeff_subst_eq_finsum
declarations.signature: theorem coeff_subst_eq_finsum {g : S⟦X⟧} (hg : HasSubst g) (f : R⟦X⟧) (n : ℕ) :

declarations.doc: **Finite-support collapse.**  When the substituted series `g` has zero constant coefficient, the coefficient of the substitution `coeff n (subst g f)` is a *finite* sum over `d ∈ range (n+1)`, because the higher powers `g^d` (`d > n`) contribute zero.  This is the precise sense in which the degree-`n` coefficient of `γ = subst g (mk α)` depends on only the first `n+1` coefficients of `f` (here `f = mk α`).
declarations.name: ArkLib.coeff_subst_eq_sum_range
declarations.signature: theorem coeff_subst_eq_sum_range {g : S⟦X⟧} (hg : constantCoeff (R := S) g = 0) (f : R⟦X⟧) (n : ℕ) :

declarations.doc: The degree-`n` coefficient of `subst g f` only sees the first `n+1` coefficients of `f`: if two power series agree on `coeff 0 … coeff n`, their substitutions agree on `coeff n`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.coeff_subst_congr_of_coeff_eq
declarations.signature: theorem coeff_subst_congr_of_coeff_eq {g : S⟦X⟧} (hg : constantCoeff (R := S) g = 0) {f₁ f₂ : R⟦X⟧}

declarations.doc: **Truncation commutes with substitution (coefficientwise).**  For `g` with zero constant coefficient, the degree-`< k` coefficients of `subst g f` equal those of `subst g (trunc k f)`: the truncated `γ` depends only on the first `k` coefficients of `f = mk α`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.coeff_subst_eq_coeff_subst_coe_trunc
declarations.signature: theorem coeff_subst_eq_coeff_subst_coe_trunc {g : S⟦X⟧} (hg : constantCoeff (R := S) g = 0) (f : R⟦X⟧)

declarations.doc: **Truncation commutes with substitution.**  For `g` with zero constant coefficient, the length-`k` truncation of `subst g f` equals the length-`k` truncation of `subst g (trunc k f)`. Equivalently, the degree-`< k` part of `γ = subst g (mk α)` is computed from the polynomial `trunc k (mk α)`, i.e. depends only on `α 0, …, α (k-1)`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.trunc_subst_eq_trunc_subst_coe_trunc
declarations.signature: theorem trunc_subst_eq_trunc_subst_coe_trunc {g : S⟦X⟧} (hg : constantCoeff (R := S) g = 0) (f : R⟦X⟧)

declarations.doc: If all coefficients of `f` of degree `≥ k` vanish, then `f` equals the coercion of its own length-`k` truncation (a polynomial of degree `< k`).  This is the power-series identity behind "the truncated `γ` is a polynomial of the stated degree".
declarations.name: ArkLib.eq_coe_trunc_of_coeff_eq_zero

declarations.doc: **Tail-vanishing ⟹ substitution of a polynomial.**  If `α t = 0` for all `t ≥ k`, then `γ = subst g f` equals `subst g (trunc k f)`, i.e. the substitution of an explicit polynomial of degree `< k`.  This is the power-series side of Claim 5.8' (the truncation) packaged so Claim 5.9 can read `degreeX P ≤ 1` off the polynomial `trunc k f`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.subst_eq_subst_coe_trunc_of_coeff_eq_zero

declarations.doc: The substitution of a polynomial `p` into `g` is the polynomial-`aeval` of `g`, so `subst g (trunc k f)` is literally `Polynomial.aeval g (trunc k f)`.  Combined with `subst_eq_subst_coe_trunc_of_coeff_eq_zero`, this exhibits `γ` (under tail-vanishing) as a finite algebraic expression in `g` with polynomial coefficients drawn from `α 0, …, α (k-1)`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.subst_coe_trunc_eq_aeval
declarations.signature: theorem subst_coe_trunc_eq_aeval {g : S⟦X⟧} (hg : HasSubst g) (f : R⟦X⟧) (k : ℕ) :

declarations.doc: **Tail-vanishing ⟹ `γ` is `aeval` of the truncation polynomial.**  Composite of the previous two lemmas, in the single form most useful downstream: if `coeff t f = 0` for `t ≥ k`, then `subst g f = Polynomial.aeval g (trunc k f)`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.subst_eq_aeval_trunc_of_coeff_eq_zero
declarations.signature: theorem subst_eq_aeval_trunc_of_coeff_eq_zero {g : S⟦X⟧} (hg : HasSubst g) {f : R⟦X⟧} {k : ℕ}

declarations.doc: The application-shaped restatement of `eq_coe_trunc_of_coeff_eq_zero` for `f = mk α`: if `α t = 0` for all `t ≥ k`, then `mk α` is the polynomial `trunc k (mk α)`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.mk_eq_coe_trunc_of_tail_zero
declarations.signature: theorem mk_eq_coe_trunc_of_tail_zero {α : ℕ → R} {k : ℕ} (hα : ∀ t, k ≤ t → α t = 0) :

declarations.doc: The application-shaped restatement of `subst_eq_aeval_trunc_of_coeff_eq_zero` for the actual `γ = subst g (mk α)`: if `α t = 0` for all `t ≥ k`, then `γ = Polynomial.aeval g (trunc k (mk α))`, the `aeval` of an explicit degree-`< k` polynomial whose coefficients are exactly `α 0, …, α (k-1)`.  This is precisely the form Claims 5.8'/5.9 consume (`degreeX P ≤ 1` is then a statement about `trunc k (mk α)`).
declarations.name: ArkLib.subst_mk_eq_aeval_trunc_of_tail_zero
declarations.signature: theorem subst_mk_eq_aeval_trunc_of_tail_zero {g : S⟦X⟧} (hg : HasSubst g) {α : ℕ → R} {k : ℕ}

declarations.doc: The truncation polynomial of `mk α` has `natDegree < k` (for `k > 0`): the explicit degree bound on the polynomial extracted above.
declarations.name: ArkLib.natDegree_trunc_mk_lt
declarations.signature: theorem natDegree_trunc_mk_lt {α : ℕ → R} {k : ℕ} (hk : 0 < k) :

declarations.doc: The `n`-th polynomial coefficient of `trunc k (mk α)` is `α n` for `n < k`, and `0` otherwise: the truncation polynomial's coefficients are exactly the first `k` entries of `α`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.coeff_trunc_mk
declarations.signature: theorem coeff_trunc_mk (α : ℕ → R) (k n : ℕ) :

declarations.doc: **The §5.9 power-series side: the truncated `γ`-numerator is linear.**  If `α t = 0` for all `t ≥ 2`, then the truncation polynomial `trunc k (mk α)` (for any `k`) is the linear polynomial `C (α 0) + C (α 1) * X`; in particular its `natDegree ≤ 1`.  This is exactly the "linear in the coefficient variable / `degreeX P ≤ 1`" bookkeeping that Claim 5.9 reads off the truncation.
declarations.name: ArkLib.trunc_mk_eq_linear_of_tail_zero
declarations.signature: theorem trunc_mk_eq_linear_of_tail_zero {α : ℕ → R} {k : ℕ} (hk : 2 ≤ k)

declarations.doc: The truncated `γ`-numerator has `natDegree ≤ 1` once the `α`-tail vanishes from index `2` on: the explicit degree bound feeding Claim 5.9's `degreeX P ≤ 1`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.natDegree_trunc_mk_le_one_of_tail_zero
declarations.signature: theorem natDegree_trunc_mk_le_one_of_tail_zero {α : ℕ → R} {k : ℕ} (hk : 2 ≤ k)

declarations.doc: The finite set of Hasse-derivative orders of total degree `< m`, drawn from a finite ambient set `amb` of exponents (in practice the construction box).  A polynomial vanishes to multiplicity `m` at a point exactly when `vanishCon p d Q = 0` for every order `d` of total degree `< m`; restricting to `amb` is harmless because only finitely many orders below the box-degree matter.
declarations.name: ArkLib.Prop55.derivsBelow
ArkLib.Prop55.mem_derivsBelow
declarations.signature: lemma mem_derivsBelow {amb : Finset (Fin 3 →₀ ℕ)} {m : ℕ} {d : Fin 3 →₀ ℕ} :

declarations.doc: **Prop-5.5, interpolant half.**  Given a finite point set `pts` (the close-codeword graphs of §5), a multiplicity `m`, and a construction box `box`, the GS-count inequality `#pts · #(derivsBelow box m) < #box` yields a non-zero box-supported trivariate polynomial `Q` vanishing to multiplicity `< m` at every point.  Discharged entirely by the verified engine `GS.exists_gs_interpolant`; **no extraction hypothesis is needed for this half**.
declarations.name: ArkLib.Prop55.exists_interpolant

declarations.doc: A matching-polynomial *extractor* for a point set: from the GS interpolant `Q` it returns, for each point, a matching polynomial satisfying the extraction property `prop`.  This is the GS-factorization datum that Prop 5.5 consumes.  It is supplied by the §5 root-clearing / factorization machinery (`RootClearing.lean`), not proven here — we make it the explicit isolated hypothesis so the rest of Prop 5.5 is unconditional.
declarations.name: ArkLib.Prop55.MatchingExtractor

declarations.doc: **Proposition 5.5 of [BCIKS20]** (`exists_a_set_and_a_matching_polynomial`), standalone. There exists a *set* (the non-zero, box-supported, multiplicity-`m`-vanishing interpolant `Q` together with its point set `pts`) and, for each point, a *matching polynomial* `g`. The statement is reduced to exactly two inputs: * `hcount` — the GS-count numeric inequality `#pts · #(derivsBelow box m) < #box` (the Johnson-radius parameter condition of §5); and * `extract` — the matching-polynomial extraction predicate `MatchingExtractor`, the GS list-decoding factorization datum. The interpolant-existence ha
declarations.name: ArkLib.Prop55.exists_a_set_and_a_matching_polynomial

declarations.doc: The pure interpolant core of Prop 5.5: with only the GS-count inequality, there is a non-zero box-supported interpolant vanishing to multiplicity `m`.  No extraction hypothesis. This is the half the §5 graph-vanishing bridge (`Q_vanishes_on_close_codeword_graph`) ultimately rests on.
declarations.name: ArkLib.Prop55.exists_a_matching_interpolant

declarations.doc: The `intDegree` of a constant (image of a field element) is `0`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.intDegree_algebraMap_field

declarations.doc: `intDegree` is additive under powers (for nonzero base): `intDegree (a ^ n) = n • intDegree a`. This is the exact propagation rule for the multiplicative part of the Hensel recursion.
declarations.name: ArkLib.intDegree_pow
declarations.signature: theorem intDegree_pow {a : RatFunc F} (ha : a ≠ 0) (n : ℕ) :

declarations.doc: `zDeg r` is the *size* of the reduced representation of `r`, namely the maximum of the `natDegree`s of its numerator and denominator.  For `r = p/q` in lowest terms, `zDeg r = max (deg p) (deg q)`; this is the quantity one must bound to control the storage cost of a rational function during the BCIKS20 Hensel lifting.
declarations.name: ArkLib.zDeg
declarations.signature: def zDeg (r : RatFunc F) : ℕ := max r.num.natDegree r.denom.natDegree

declarations.name: ArkLib.zDeg_def
declarations.signature: theorem zDeg_def (r : RatFunc F) : zDeg r = max r.num.natDegree r.denom.natDegree := rfl

declarations.name: ArkLib.num_natDegree_le_zDeg
declarations.signature: theorem num_natDegree_le_zDeg (r : RatFunc F) : r.num.natDegree ≤ zDeg r := le_max_left _ _

declarations.name: ArkLib.denom_natDegree_le_zDeg
declarations.signature: theorem denom_natDegree_le_zDeg (r : RatFunc F) : r.denom.natDegree ≤ zDeg r := le_max_right _ _

declarations.name: ArkLib.zDeg_zero
declarations.signature: theorem zDeg_zero : zDeg (0 : RatFunc F) = 0 := by

declarations.name: ArkLib.zDeg_one
declarations.signature: theorem zDeg_one : zDeg (1 : RatFunc F) = 0 := by

declarations.doc: The size of a constant (image of a field element under `algebraMap`) is `0`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.zDeg_algebraMap
declarations.signature: theorem zDeg_algebraMap (c : F) : zDeg (algebraMap F (RatFunc F) c) = 0 := by

declarations.doc: The size of a polynomial constant `RatFunc.C c` is `0`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.zDeg_C
declarations.signature: theorem zDeg_C (c : F) : zDeg (RatFunc.C c) = 0 := by

declarations.doc: Submultiplicativity of `zDeg`: `zDeg (a * b) ≤ zDeg a + zDeg b`. The proof uses the mathlib divisibility facts `RatFunc.num_mul_dvd` and `RatFunc.denom_mul_dvd` together with `Polynomial.natDegree_le_of_dvd`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.zDeg_mul_le
declarations.signature: theorem zDeg_mul_le (a b : RatFunc F) : zDeg (a * b) ≤ zDeg a + zDeg b := by

declarations.doc: The common-denominator identity for `RatFunc F`: writing `a` and `b` over their reduced denominators and combining the fractions, `a + b` equals `(a.num * b.denom + a.denom * b.num) / (a.denom * b.denom)` as images of polynomials.
declarations.name: ArkLib.add_eq_combined_div

declarations.doc: The numerator of a sum divides the "common denominator" combination `a.num * b.denom + a.denom * b.num`, provided the sum is nonzero. This is the additive analogue of `RatFunc.num_mul_dvd` and is the key step for the addition bound; it is not in mathlib so we prove it via `RatFunc.num_dvd`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.num_add_dvd

declarations.doc: Subadditivity of `zDeg`: `zDeg (a + b) ≤ zDeg a + zDeg b`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.zDeg_add_le
declarations.signature: theorem zDeg_add_le (a b : RatFunc F) : zDeg (a + b) ≤ zDeg a + zDeg b := by

declarations.doc: The weaker, explicitly-requested addition bound `zDeg (a + b) ≤ max (zDeg a) (zDeg b) + zDeg b`, an immediate consequence of `zDeg_add_le`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.zDeg_add_le'
declarations.signature: theorem zDeg_add_le' (a b : RatFunc F) : zDeg (a + b) ≤ max (zDeg a) (zDeg b) + zDeg b :=

declarations.doc: The size measure is invariant under inversion: `zDeg a⁻¹ = zDeg a`. Inversion swaps numerator and denominator up to a unit (`RatFunc.associated_num_inv` / `RatFunc.associated_denom_inv`); associated polynomials have equal `natDegree`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.zDeg_inv
declarations.signature: theorem zDeg_inv (a : RatFunc F) : zDeg a⁻¹ = zDeg a := by

declarations.doc: Powers grow the size measure at most linearly: `zDeg (a ^ n) ≤ n * zDeg a`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.zDeg_pow_le
declarations.signature: theorem zDeg_pow_le (a : RatFunc F) (n : ℕ) : zDeg (a ^ n) ≤ n * zDeg a := by

declarations.doc: `zpow` (integer power) of a nonzero rational function grows the size measure linearly in `|n|`: `zDeg (a ^ n) ≤ n.natAbs * zDeg a`.  Combines `zDeg_pow_le` with `zDeg_inv`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.zDeg_zpow_le
declarations.signature: theorem zDeg_zpow_le {a : RatFunc F} (n : ℤ) : zDeg (a ^ n) ≤ n.natAbs * zDeg a := by

declarations.doc: When `β` is a nonzero regular element, the set `S_β β` of matching specialization points is finite: it is contained in the (finite) root set of the nonzero resultant `Res_Y(canonicalRepOf𝒪 hH β, H_tilde' H)`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.S_β_finite_of_ne_zero

declarations.doc: A `Finset` of matching points sitting inside `S_β β` has cardinality bounded by the `ncard` of `S_β β`, provided `β ≠ 0` (which guarantees `S_β β` is finite).
declarations.name: ArkLib.finset_card_le_ncard_S_β

declarations.doc: **Packaging form of `Lemma_A_1` from a numeric `ncard` lower bound.** If `S_β β` has at least `N` matching points (`N ≤ (S_β β).ncard`) and `N` already beats the bound `weight_Λ_over_𝒪 hH β D * H.natDegree` (inequality in `WithBot ℕ`), then the embedding of `β` into the function field `𝕃` is zero.
declarations.name: ArkLib.embedding_eq_zero_of_ncard_lower_bound

declarations.doc: **Packaging form of `Lemma_A_1` from a `Finset` of matching points.** The eventual ingredient-C application only needs to exhibit a `Finset F` `T` with `↑T ⊆ S_β β` whose cardinality exceeds the weight bound `weight_Λ_over_𝒪 hH β D * H.natDegree` (in `WithBot ℕ`). This brick discharges everything else, concluding `embeddingOf𝒪Into𝕃 _ β = 0` (which feeds `alpha'_eq_zero_of_embedding_beta_eq_zero` → Claim 5.8).
declarations.name: ArkLib.embedding_eq_zero_of_finset_subset_S_β

declarations.doc: **Siegel lemma (finite-dimensional form).**  A linear map `T : V →ₗ[K] W` between finite-dimensional `K`-vector spaces whose target has strictly smaller dimension than its source has a non-trivial kernel: there exists `v ≠ 0` with `T v = 0`.  This is the linear-algebra heart of the Guruswami–Sudan interpolant existence argument (more unknowns than constraints ⟹ a non-zero solution).
declarations.name: ArkLib.siegel_exists_nonzero
declarations.signature: theorem siegel_exists_nonzero {K V W : Type*} [Field K]

declarations.doc: The constraint linear map of a matrix `a : C → M → K`, sending a coefficient vector `c : M → K` to the vector of constraint values `j ↦ ∑ i, c i * a j i`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.constraintMatrixMap
declarations.signature: def constraintMatrixMap {K : Type*} [Field K] {M C : Type*} [Fintype M]

declarations.name: ArkLib.constraintMatrixMap_apply
declarations.signature: lemma constraintMatrixMap_apply {K : Type*} [Field K] {M C : Type*}

declarations.doc: **Guruswami–Sudan counting corollary (matrix form).**  Given a finite set `M` of "monomials", a finite set `C` of linear "constraints" with `#C < #M`, and a constraint matrix `a : C → M → K`, there is a non-zero coefficient vector `c : M → K` satisfying every constraint `∑ i, c i * a j i = 0`.  Equivalently: the kernel of the constraint matrix `(M → K) →ₗ[K] (C → K)` is non-trivial.
declarations.name: ArkLib.exists_nonzero_constraint_solution
declarations.signature: theorem exists_nonzero_constraint_solution {K : Type*} [Field K] {M C : Type*}

declarations.doc: The `K`-linear "Hasse-derivative" functional of order `d` at the point `p`: it reads the `d`-th coefficient of the translate `Q(X + p)` of `Q`.  A polynomial `Q` vanishes to multiplicity `m` at `p` iff `vanishCon p d Q = 0` for every order `d` of total degree `< m`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.GS.vanishCon
declarations.signature: def vanishCon {K : Type*} [CommRing K] (p : Fin 3 → K)

declarations.doc: The polynomial `∑_{d ∈ box} c d · X^d` built from a coefficient vector on a degree box, as a `K`-linear map in the coefficients.
declarations.name: ArkLib.GS.polyOfLin

declarations.doc: The coefficient of `polyOfLin box c` at a box exponent `d` is exactly `c d`.

declarations.signature: lemma coeff_polyOfLin {K : Type*} [CommRing K] (box : Finset (Fin 3 →₀ ℕ))

declarations.signature: lemma support_polyOfLin {K : Type*} [CommRing K] (box : Finset (Fin 3 →₀ ℕ))

declarations.signature: lemma polyOfLin_ne_zero {K : Type*} [CommRing K] (box : Finset (Fin 3 →₀ ℕ))

declarations.name: ArkLib.GS.consMap

declarations.doc: **Guruswami–Sudan interpolant — coefficient form.**  If a degree box has strictly more monomials than there are vanishing constraints, then there is a non-zero coefficient vector on the box whose polynomial satisfies every constraint.  (`vanishCon p d (polyOfLin box c) = 0` is the order-`d` vanishing condition at the point `p`.)
declarations.name: ArkLib.GS.exists_nonzero_box_coeffs

declarations.doc: **Guruswami–Sudan interpolant — polynomial form.**  Under the GS count `cons.card < box.card`, there is a non-zero polynomial `Q : MvPolynomial (Fin 3) K` supported on the degree box that satisfies every vanishing constraint.
declarations.name: ArkLib.GS.exists_nonzero_box_poly

declarations.doc: **Guruswami–Sudan multiplicity-`m` interpolant.**  Let `pts` be a finite set of points in `K^3` and `derivs` a finite set of derivative orders.  For genuine multiplicity-`m` vanishing one takes `derivs = {d | d.degree < m}`, so that `derivs.card` is the number of order-`< m` Hasse derivatives in three variables. If the degree box has more monomials than the total number of constraints, `pts.card * derivs.card < box.card`, then there is a non-zero box-supported polynomial `Q` with `vanishCon p d Q = 0` for every point `p ∈ pts` and every order `d ∈ derivs` — i.e. `Q` vanishes (to the prescribed
declarations.name: ArkLib.GS.exists_gs_interpolant

declarations.signature: lemma lemma.  By `hasSubst_shiftSeries_iff_eq_zero` below this hypothesis is *equivalent to* `x₀ = 0`, so

declarations.doc: `PowerSeries.HasSubst g` unfolds to `IsNilpotent (constantCoeff g)`; we record the convenient `PowerSeries.constantCoeff` form (defeq via the `Unit`-indexed `MvPowerSeries`).
declarations.name: ArkLib.SubstFieldCaveat.hasSubst_iff_isNilpotent_constantCoeff
declarations.signature: theorem hasSubst_iff_isNilpotent_constantCoeff {S : Type*} [CommRing S] (g : PowerSeries S) :

declarations.doc: **The field caveat.**  Over a field `K`, a power series may be substituted into iff its constant coefficient vanishes: `HasSubst g ↔ constantCoeff g = 0`.  (A field is reduced, so nilpotent ⇔ zero.) This is the precise sense in which `PowerSeries.subst` over a field needs a "positive order" series: if `constantCoeff g ≠ 0`, `HasSubst g` is false and `subst g · ` is mathlib's junk default.
declarations.name: ArkLib.SubstFieldCaveat.hasSubst_iff_constantCoeff_eq_zero_of_field

declarations.doc: Contrapositive convenience form: a nonzero constant coefficient over a field obstructs substitution.
declarations.name: ArkLib.SubstFieldCaveat.not_hasSubst_of_constantCoeff_ne_zero_of_field

declarations.doc: The BCIKS shift series `X ↦ X − x₀` underlying the in-tree `γ`: `shift 0 = fieldTo𝕃 (-x₀)`, `shift 1 = 1`, `shift t = 0` for `t ≥ 2`. This is the `PowerSeries.mk subst` appearing literally in `γ` (`RationalFunctions.lean:2886`); it matches `Claim59Conditional.shiftSeries`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.SubstFieldCaveat.shiftSeries
declarations.signature: def shiftSeries (x₀ : F) (H : F[X][Y]) : PowerSeries (𝕃 H) :=

declarations.doc: The constant coefficient of the BCIKS shift series is `fieldTo𝕃 (-x₀)`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.SubstFieldCaveat.constantCoeff_shiftSeries

declarations.doc: `fieldTo𝕃` is injective: it is a ring hom out of the field `F` into the nontrivial field `𝕃 H` (nontrivial because `H` is irreducible of positive `Y`-degree).
declarations.name: ArkLib.SubstFieldCaveat.fieldTo𝕃_injective

declarations.doc: `fieldTo𝕃 (-x₀) = 0 ↔ x₀ = 0`, by injectivity of `fieldTo𝕃`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.SubstFieldCaveat.fieldTo𝕃_neg_eq_zero_iff

declarations.doc: **Corollary (the in-tree `γ` caveat).**  The BCIKS substitution `X ↦ X − x₀` underlying the in-tree `γ` has zero constant coefficient — and hence satisfies mathlib's `HasSubst` over the field `𝕃 H` — **iff `x₀ = 0`**.  For `x₀ ≠ 0` the substitution is invalid and the in-tree `γ = subst (mk shift) (mk α)` is mathlib's junk default.
declarations.name: ArkLib.SubstFieldCaveat.hasSubst_shiftSeries_iff_eq_zero

declarations.doc: The centred case: `HasSubst (shiftSeries 0 H)` always holds (the shift series is then `X`).
declarations.name: ArkLib.SubstFieldCaveat.hasSubst_shiftSeries_zero

declarations.doc: The off-centre case: for `x₀ ≠ 0` the substitution underlying the in-tree `γ` is **invalid**.
declarations.name: ArkLib.SubstFieldCaveat.not_hasSubst_shiftSeries_of_ne_zero

declarations.doc: The `𝒪`-weight of `a` is bounded by the polynomial weight of its own canonical representative (in fact they are equal by definition); recorded as a `≤` for uniform downstream use.
declarations.name: ArkLib.weight_Λ_over_𝒪_le_canonicalRep

declarations.doc: The canonical representative is a `mk`-preimage of the element it represents, so it is a valid witness for `weight_Λ_over_𝒪_le_of_mk_eq`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.weight_Λ_over_𝒪_eq_canonicalRep

declarations.doc: The `𝒪`-weight of `0` is `⊥` (restated from the in-tree `weight_Λ_over_𝒪_zero`).
declarations.name: ArkLib.weight_Λ_over_𝒪_zero'

declarations.doc: The `𝒪`-weight of `1` is `≤ 0`: the constant `1` has a degree-`0` `F[X]`-coefficient and no `Y`-power contribution.
declarations.name: ArkLib.weight_Λ_over_𝒪_one_le

declarations.doc: Sub-additivity of the `𝒪`-weight under addition: `Λ(a + b) ≤ max (Λ a) (Λ b)`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.weight_Λ_over_𝒪_add_le

declarations.doc: Sub-additivity of the `𝒪`-weight under negation: it is invariant.
declarations.name: ArkLib.weight_Λ_over_𝒪_neg
declarations.signature: lemma weight_Λ_over_𝒪_neg {H : F[X][Y]} {D : ℕ} (hH : 0 < H.natDegree) (a : 𝒪 H) :

declarations.doc: Sub-additivity of the `𝒪`-weight under subtraction: `Λ(a - b) ≤ max (Λ a) (Λ b)`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.weight_Λ_over_𝒪_sub_le

declarations.doc: The `𝒪`-weight of a finite sum is bounded by the `sup` of the summands' weights.
declarations.name: ArkLib.weight_Λ_over_𝒪_sum_le

declarations.doc: Sub-multiplicativity of the `𝒪`-weight: `Λ(a · b) ≤ Λ a + Λ b`. This is the central inequality the App.-A.4 induction telescopes.
declarations.name: ArkLib.weight_Λ_over_𝒪_mul_le

declarations.doc: Sub-multiplicativity for powers: `Λ(a ^ n) ≤ n • Λ a` (with `0 • Λ a = 0`, matching `weight_Λ_over_𝒪_one_le`).
declarations.name: ArkLib.weight_Λ_over_𝒪_pow_le

declarations.doc: The `𝒪`-weight of the image of a scalar `C c` is bounded by `c.natDegree`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.weight_Λ_over_𝒪_C_le

declarations.doc: A scalar multiple `mk (C c) * a` raises the weight by at most `c.natDegree`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.weight_Λ_over_𝒪_C_mul_le

declarations.doc: The regular element `W = mk (C H.leadingCoeff)` realizing `liftToFunctionField H.leadingCoeff`. This is the in-tree `W` of Claim A.2 as an element of `𝒪 H`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.W_reg

declarations.doc: `W_reg` indeed maps to `liftToFunctionField H.leadingCoeff = W` in `𝕃 H`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.embeddingOf𝒪Into𝕃_W_reg

declarations.doc: `Λ(W) ≤ D − d_H` (the `Λ(W)` bound of App.-A A.2): the weight of the regular `W` is bounded by the `F[X]`-degree of the leading coefficient, which is `≤ D − d_H` under the total-degree bound.
declarations.name: ArkLib.weight_Λ_over_𝒪_W_reg_le

declarations.doc: A `W`-multiple `W_reg * a` raises the weight by at most `D − d_H`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.weight_Λ_over_𝒪_W_reg_mul_le

declarations.doc: The `𝒪`-weight of the generator `T = mk X` is bounded by `D + 1 − d_H` (the single-`Y`-power contribution). This is the `β_0` base case of the App.-A.4 weight induction.
declarations.name: ArkLib.weight_Λ_over_𝒪_T_le

declarations.doc: The polynomial `Λ`-weight is monotone non-decreasing in the degree parameter `D`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.weight_Λ_mono_D
declarations.signature: lemma weight_Λ_mono_D {f H : F[X][Y]} {D D' : ℕ} (hDD : D ≤ D') :

declarations.doc: The `𝒪`-weight is monotone non-decreasing in the degree parameter `D`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.weight_Λ_over_𝒪_mono_D
declarations.signature: lemma weight_Λ_over_𝒪_mono_D {H : F[X][Y]} (hH : 0 < H.natDegree) (a : 𝒪 H)

declarations.doc: If `Λ a ≤ (ba : WithBot ℕ)` and `Λ b ≤ (bb : WithBot ℕ)` then `Λ(a·b) ≤ (ba + bb : ℕ)`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.weight_Λ_over_𝒪_mul_le_of_le

declarations.doc: If `Λ a ≤ (ba : WithBot ℕ)` and `Λ b ≤ (bb : WithBot ℕ)` then `Λ(a + b) ≤ (max ba bb : ℕ)`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.weight_Λ_over_𝒪_add_le_of_le

declarations.doc: If `Λ a ≤ (b : WithBot ℕ)` then `Λ(a ^ n) ≤ (n · b : ℕ)`.
declarations.name: ArkLib.weight_Λ_over_𝒪_pow_le_of_le

declarations.doc: The `(2t+1)·d_R·D` weight target of Claim A.2 as a `WithBot ℕ`-bound transfers along `≤`: if a witness's weight is `≤` the tight `1 + (t+1)Λ(W) + e_t·Λ(ξ)` budget and that budget is `≤ (2t+1)·d_R·D`, the Claim A.2 bound holds. This is the `L10`-collapse shape.
declarations.name: ArkLib.weight_Λ_over_𝒪_le_trans_nat
declarations.signature: lemma weight_Λ_over_𝒪_le_trans_nat {H : F[X][Y]} {D : ℕ} {a : 𝒪 H} {b c : ℕ}

declarations.doc: `simulateQ` distributes over `forIn` on a list: a monad morphism commutes with `forIn`. Proved by list induction via `List.forIn_cons` + `simulateQ_bind`, casing on the `ForInStep` accumulator. (Verbatim copy of the upstream VCVio lemma; delete on the next VCVio bump.)
declarations.name: simulateQ_list_forIn
declarations.signature: lemma simulateQ_list_forIn {ι : Type*} {spec : OracleSpec ι} {n : Type → Type _}

declarations.doc: Resolve a `simulateQ` over a three-way `addLift impl (impl₁ + impl₂)` applied to a computation `x : OracleComp spec₁ α` that has been double-`liftM`'d — first into the inner sum `spec₁ + spec₂`, then into the outer sum `spec + (spec₁ + spec₂)`. The query routes to the *left* inner implementation `impl₁`, leaving `liftM (simulateQ impl₁ x)`. This is the `left` half of the `simOracle2`-routing pair: it peels the outer `addLift` (`simulateQ_add_liftComp_right`), commutes the inner `simulateQ` past the target lift (`simulateQ_liftTarget`), then peels the inner sum (`simulateQ_add_liftComp_left`).
declarations.name: simulateQ_addLift_add_liftM_left

declarations.doc: Resolve a `simulateQ` over a three-way `addLift impl (impl₁ + impl₂)` applied to a computation `x : OracleComp spec₂ α` that has been double-`liftM`'d — first into the inner sum `spec₁ + spec₂`, then into the outer sum `spec + (spec₁ + spec₂)`. The query routes to the *right* inner implementation `impl₂`, leaving `liftM (simulateQ impl₂ x)`. The `right` companion of `simulateQ_addLift_add_liftM_left`; see that lemma for the `simOracle2` motivation.
declarations.name: simulateQ_addLift_add_liftM_right

declarations.name: StateT.run'_map_comm
